courses.subject: SEEM
courses.title: Operations Research I
courses.description: This course provides an introduction to operations research as a quantitative approach to decision-making. Students learn how to formulate real-world problems as mathematical optimization models, with a primary focus on linear and integer programming. The course also covers classic network and logistics models, including transportation, transshipment, assignment, shortest path, traveling salesman, vehicle routing, and multi-commodity flow problems, as well as an introduction to large-scale solution techniques such as column generation and constraint generation. Throughout the course, students use Excel and Python-based solvers to implement models and interpret results, connecting mathematical solutions with managerial or engineering insights.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken ESTR2520 or FTEC2101.
courses.academic_group: Dept of Sys Engg & Engg Mgt
courses.academic_org: Dept of Sys Engg & Engg Mgt
courses.course_syllabus:
Week 1: Introduction to Operations Research.

Week 2: Linear Programming: Modelling examples.

Week 3: Linear Programming: Modelling examples and graphical solutions.

Week 4: Linear Programming: Excel implementation and sensitivity analysis.

Week 5: Linear Programming: Simplex method and Python implementation.

Week 6: Advanced Modelling using LP: Linear fractional problems, linearization techniques.

Week 7: Integer Programming: Modelling examples.

Week 8: Integer Programming: Branch-and-Bounds and Heuristics.

Week 9: Network Flows: Transportation, transhipment and assignment problem.

Week 10: Shortest Path Problem, Travelling Salesman, Vehicle Routing Problem.

Week 11: Multi-commodity Network Flows.

Week 12: Large-scale solution methods (column generation, constraint generation)

Week 13: Course review
courses.required_readings: Lecture notes and slides distributed by the course instructors.
courses.recommended_readings:
1. Bernard Taylor III, Introduction to Management Science, 13th Edition, Pearson 2019.

2. F.S. Hillier and G.J. Lieberman, Introduction to Operations Research, 9th Edition, McGraw-Hill, 2010.

courses.description:
This course presents an introductory roadmap into the newly emerged and rapidly evolving field of data science, with the objective of introducing the problem-solving mindset in a data-intensive context.
The course projects data science as a productive synthesis of its parent disciplines, including mathematics, statistics, computing, data mining, system science and data visualization, etc. Such a productive synthesis is applicable across many fields to bring about scientific discovery through data-intensive, analytical methods. This course aims to help navigate students in taking more advanced courses in its parent disciplines to build up their expertise in data science. We cover topics including:
History and impact of Data Science.
Collecting Data: sources, types and categorization of data.
Visualising Data: summary statistics, data display, data dictionaries, schema and graphical visualization.
Analysing Data: pattern recognition, correlations and relationships, hypotheses testing, statistical significance.
Investigating Data: data mining, machine learning, inference, meta-data, modeling, eliciting meaning and validation.
Applicational contexts: Examples of useful applications from case studies.

courses.subject: SEEM
courses.title: System Modeling and Simulation
courses.description: Systems such as hospitals, call centers, airports, and urban logistics networks face inherent uncertainty in demand and resource availability. This course introduces modeling and simulation techniques for analyzing and improving such systems. Students will learn the relationships among systems, models, and simulations, and study canonical queueing models. The course emphasizes the use of discrete-event simulation in Python to evaluate and optimize system performance under stochastic variability. Topics include system modeling, analytical performance measures, simulation fundamentals, model calibration, design of experiments for system improvement, domain-specific case studies, and an introduction to how simulation interacts with artificial intelligence (AI).
courses.enrollment_requirement: Prerequisites: SEEM2430 or ENGG2430 or ENGG2450 or ESTR2002 or ESTR2005 or (ENGG2760/ESTR2018 and ENGG2780/ESTR2020) or with the approval of the course instructor.
courses.academic_group: Dept of Sys Engg & Engg Mgt
courses.academic_org: Dept of Sys Engg & Engg Mgt
courses.learning_outcomes:
By the end of the class, students will be able to:

1. Explain the relationships among *system*, *model*, and *simulation*, and identify modeling assumptions in service systems.
2. Understand canonical queueing models for service systems (M/M/1, M/M/c, M/M/c/c, M/M/1+M, G/G/1) and their performance measures.
3. Implement discrete-event simulations in Python to evaluate service systems, including steady‑state detection and uncertainty quantification.
4. Understand foundational simulation theory, including random number generator, variance reduction, and rare-event simulation.
5. Calibrate models using historical data, and validate simulated results against empirical or analytical benchmarks.
6. Apply simulation-based experiment (factorial design and scenario analysis) to guide system improvement.
7. Model and analyze real-world service systems such as hospitals, call centers, and airports operations.
8. Explain the interplay between simulation and AI.
9. Communicate operational recommendations to stakeholders with clear visual analytics and risk narratives.
courses.course_syllabus:
* *W1 – Concepts of system v.s. model v.s. simulation, and basic queueing models**: flows, entities, and processes; Kendall notation; KPIs (queue length, waiting time, throughput, utilization); Little's law

* *W2 – Analytical and simulated queue behavior**: study and compare analytical Erlang formulas (queue lengths, waiting time, and utilization) with simulated results; interpret differences

* *W3 – Discrete-event simulation fundamentals (Part 1: Application)**: Event-based simulation principle (time advance, states, events, clock management), Python implementation, steady‑state detection (Welch method), confidence interval construction (replications or batch means)

* *W4 – Discrete-event simulation fundamentals (Part 2: Theory)**: Random number generator (linear congruential generators, transform methods), variance reduction (antithetic variates, control variates), rare-event simulation (importance sampling)

* *W5 – Model calibration from empirical data**: fit input distributions from empirical data using Python (Goodness-of-fit tests, moment matching, parametric distributions and MLE estimation); discuss failure of Erlang formulas for general queues

* *W6– Design of experiments for system improvement**: factorial/screening designs; response surfaces; metamodels; common random numbers.

* *W7 – Hospital operations (Case 1)**: ED patient flow; bed management; triage priority;

* *W8 – Call center staffing & scheduling (Case 2)**: shift design; skill‑based routing; resource pooling.

* *W9 – Airport design (Case 3)**: check‑in, priority line, security, immigration, boarding

* *W10 – Simulation and AI (An Int****roduction****)**: simulation for AI (provide training data, test AI safety, provide tools for AI agents); AI for simulation (synthetic data generation, simulation acceleration via metamodel learning, behavior modeling in agent-based simulation)

* *W11 – Case study clinics**: peer review and stress‑testing of projects.

* *W12 – Project presentations & policy briefs**.

courses.subject: SEEM
courses.title: Operations Research II
courses.description: Non-linear programming: convex sets and functions; local and global optima; Lagrange multipliers; optimality conditions for unconstrained problems; descent methods; constrained optimization; Karush-Kuhn-Tucker conditions; solution methods; stochastic optimization. Dynamic programming: models and formulation; Bellman's equations; solution methods. Reinforcement learning: tabular MDP; policy evaluation/improvement; connection to DP.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken ESTR2520 or ESTR3500 or FTEC2101.
courses.academic_group: Dept of Sys Engg & Engg Mgt
courses.academic_org: Dept of Sys Engg & Engg Mgt
courses.learning_outcomes:
At the completion of the course, the students should have the ability to:
1. Model unconstrained and constrained nonlinear programs;

2. Apply first‑order methods and KKT conditions; recognize convexity and use convex modeling tools when available.
3. Build dynamic programming models (finite‑horizon, inventory, knapsack variants) and understand curse‑of‑dimensionality trade‑offs.

4. Implement reinforcement learning (tabular MDPs; policy evaluation; Q‑learning) and formulate stochastic optimization problems.
courses.course_syllabus:
Week 1: Nonlinear Optimization Theory: Convex Set, Gradients.

Week 2: Nonlinear Optimization Theory: Constraint Qualification, KKT Conditions.

Week 3: Algorithms for Unconstrained Nonlinear Optimization: Gradient Method, Newton Method, Quasi-Newton Method.

Week 4: Algorithms for Constrained Nonlinear Optimization: Projected Method, ADMM Method, Primal-dual Method.

Week 5: Modelling and Formulation of Convex Problems: Solving Quadratic Program.

Week 6: Modelling and Formulation of Convex Problems: SDP Relaxation for Non-convex Optimization.

Week 7: Stochastic Programming: Risk, Regret, Two-stage Optimization with Recourse, Sample Average Approximation.

Week 8: Stochastic Programming: Stochastic Gradient Method, Stochastic Algorithms.

Week 9: Dynamic Programming 1: State/Actions, Bellman Equation, Knapsack DP.

Week 10: Dynamic Programming 2: Approximate DP, Value Function Approximation.

Week 11: Reinforcement Learning: Policy Evaluation/Improvement, Q-Learning.

Week 12: Integrated Case Study.

2. D.G. Luenberger and Y.Y. Ye, Linear and Nonlinear Programming, 3rd Edition, Springer, 2008.

3. S. Boyd and L. Vandenberghe, Convex Optimization, Cambridge University Press, 2004.

4. D.P. Bertsekas, Dynamic Programming and Optimal Control, Athena Scientific, 2017.

courses.subject: SEEM
courses.title: Introduction to Logistics and Supply Chain Management
courses.description:
Logistics and Supply Chain Management involves various flows (e.g., materials flow and information flow) among all of the entities that contribute value to a product, from the source of raw materials to end customers. This course will focus on the basic concepts, models and techniques for effective strategic management of an integrated and coordinated supply chain. The emphasis is on recognizing
key tradeoffs, analyzing system characteristics, and understanding effectiveness of tactics such as risk pooling, inventory policies, collaboration, and information sharing.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken ESTR3514 or ESTR4500 or ESTR4502 or SEEM4600 or SEEM4610.
courses.academic_group: Dept of Sys Engg & Engg Mgt
courses.academic_org: Dept of Sys Engg & Engg Mgt
courses.learning_outcomes:
Students will be:
1. able to generate new ideas and apply relevant techniques to improve the performance of a logistics and supply chain system.
2. able to identify engineering problems degrading the efficiency in logistics and supply chain systems and propose solutions for better management of inventory, demand, transportation, and distribution.
3. able to design and optimize a logistics and supply chain system appropriately for a particular product while achieving specified service/production targets and meeting realistic constraints.
4. competent in communicating effectively the proposed solutions to different parties in the logistics and supply chain system.
5. skilled at utilizing IT tools to analyze and implement solution methods, for example spreadsheet tools for implementing optimization methods.

6. able to introduce new concepts to management of a logistics and supply chain system due to contemporary issues such as globalization; for example, outsourcing, coordination etc.
courses.course_syllabus: This undergraduate course introduces the basic concepts/methodologies of logistics and supply chain management. In particular, we focus on the description of key tradeoffs in logistics and supply chain management, and on how to explore the optimal control strategies. Topics to be covered include: basic concepts of inventory system; Economic Ordering Quantity (EOQ) and its extension; Economic Production Quantity (EPQ); multiechelon inventory control; inventory control with time-varying demand; managing uncertainty; determining availability; beer game; supply chain coordination; revenue management; behavioral issues in supply chain; double marginalization; sourcing decisions etc.

2. Nahmias and Olsen, Production and Operations Analysis (7th Edition), 2015, Waveland Press.
3. Cachon and Terwiesch. Matching Supply with Demand: An Introduction to Operations Management, 3rd Edition, 2018, McGraw-Hill.

courses.description: This course covers the advanced topics in logistics and supply chain management. In particular, it uses comprehensive models to describe, and hence explore the optimal strategies in, various important logistics and supply chain management problems. The topics include customer service, channels of distribution, transportation, vehicle routing and scheduling, freight consolidation, facility location and network planning, storage. This course also introduces important practice in logistics and supply chain management, such as material handling systems, information systems for order processing and inventory tracking, purchasing and supply scheduling, business process re-engineering, third-party logistics, and global logistics.

Upon completion of the course, students will:

1. understand the characteristics of logistics industry in Hong Kong.
2. be familiar with the major decision issues in air, sea and land transport.
3. be able to assess a shipment/logistics/distribution solution.
4. be able to formulate a practical problem setting into a mathematical model, know how to solve it and understand the underlying assumptions and therefore its limitations and applicability.
5. understand commonly used methods for practical problems.

courses.course_syllabus: This senior undergraduate level course focuses on some advanced topics in logistics and supply chain management. Topics to be covered include: forecasting logistics and supply chain demands; facility location; distribution network design; transportation planning; storage and handling; transportation; vehicle routing and fleet; planning; flight consolidation; air/sea freight; third party logistics; RFID; green logistics; reverse logistics; case presentation etc.

courses.description: Human-computer interaction using speech and language processing draws from the perspectives of speech science, linguistics, modeling techniques and algorithms from engineering, as well as system implementations. This course presents fundamentals in speech production and perception, phonetics and phonology and signal processing techniques for speech analysis. courses.subject: SEEM
courses.title: Stochastic Calculus

meetings.location: William M W Mong Eng Bldg 406
William M W Mong Eng Bldg 406

courses.description: Introduction to continuous time stochastic processes. Brownian motions: explicit constructions, properties, quadratic variation, the Cameron-Martin-Girsanov formula, multidimensional Brownian motions. Stochastic Integration: definition, Ito’s formula, martingale representation, time change, Girsanov’s Theorem, local time and Tanaka’s formula. Stochastic differential equations and diffusion processes: strong and weak solutions, diffusions, the Feynman-Kac formula, backward stochastic differential equations. Levy processes: definition, the Levy-Khinchin representation, the Levy-Ito decomposition, the Esscher transform.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MPhil-PhD Systems Engineering and Engineering Management
courses.academic_group: Div of System Erg & Erg Mgt
courses.academic_org: Div of System Erg & Erg Mgt
courses.learning_outcomes: Students will develop an understanding of Brownian motion, stochastic integration, stochastic differential equations, diffusion processes and Levy processes. They will become familiar with the basic theory and be able to apply it to solve problems arising from applications in finance, science and engineering.
courses.course_syllabus:
This course provides a comprehensive introduction to the theory of continuous time stochastic processes (martingales, diffusions and Levy processes in particular) and stochastic calculus. Throughout the course, examples (in particular from finance) will be provided to illustrate the applications. The course consists of the following parts.

- A review of probability essentials.
- Continuous time stochastic processes; martingales.
- Brownian motions: constructions, properties, quadratic variation, the Cameron-Martin-Girsanov formula, multidimensional Brownian motions.
- Stochastic Integration: definition, Ito’s formula, change of measure, time change, martingale representation.
- Stochastic differential equations and diffusion processes: strong and weak solutions, the Feynman-Kac formula, diffusions.
- Backward stochastic differential equations: existence and uniqueness, BSDEs and PDEs.
- Levy processes: definition, the Levy-Khinchin representation, the Levy-Ito decomposition, Ito’s formula for Levy processes, the Esscher transform.
courses.required_readings: As recommended by course teacher.
courses.recommended_readings:
1. R. Cont and P. Tankov, Financial Modelling with Jump Processes, 2004, Chapman & Hall (introduction to Levy processes in a finance context).
2. I. Karatzas and S. Shreve, Brownian Motion and Stochastic Calculus, 2nd edition, 1991, Springer (a classical reference for Brownian motion and stochastic calculus).

courses.subject: SEEM
courses.title: Numerical Optimization
courses.description: This course is to teach students modern numerical optimization methods for large scale systems. Topics covered in this course include gradient method, subgradient method, proximal gradient method, Nesterov's acceleration technique, alternating direction method of multipliers, coordinate descent method, and stochastic / randomized algorithms. Applications of these optimization methods for solving problems in contemporary applications arising from big data analytics, machine learning, statistics, signal processing etc. will also be discussed.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in SEEM MPhil/PhD programme and RPg programmes under Faculty of Engineering
courses.academic_group: Div of System Erg & Erg Mgt
courses.academic_org: Div of System Erg & Erg Mgt
courses.learning_outcomes:
At the completion of this course, the student should
• familiarize with optimization algorithms for solving large scale optimization problems
• understand the convergence behaviors of different algorithms
• understand the capability of different algorithms
• be able to use learned algorithms to solve practical problems
courses.course_syllabus:
This course introduces modern numerical optimization methods and underlying theory for complex/large scale systems. Particular focuses will be given to direct search methods, various gradient methods, subgradient methods and acceleration techniques, stochastic/randomized algorithms, cutting plane methods and level function methods. It will also introduce fundamental theory of convex and quasi-convex optimization. At the end of the course, students are expected to apply the learned optimization methods to solve practical problems arising from areas such as big data analytics, machine learning, statistics and signal processing.

Syllabus (tentative)

1. Direct search methods
2. Convexity, quasi-convexity and Fenchel duality
3. Cutting plane method and level function method
4. Gradient method and quasi-Newton method
5. Subgradient and subgradient method
6. Proximal mapping and proximal gradient method
7. Accelerated proximal gradient method
8. Proximal point method and augmented Lagrangian method
9. Alternating direction method of multipliers
10. Coordinate descent method
11. Stochastic algorithms

courses.subject: SEEM
courses.title: Machine Intelligence and Systems Engineering
courses.description: This graduate level course introduces the basic concepts of machine intelligence and statistical pattern processing algorithms. A particular focus is given to state-of-the-art machine learning techniques that are used in a wide range of example application systems. These application systems are tailored for SEEM taught postgraduate students who specialize in one of two broad areas: Systems Engineering and Engineering Management; and E-Commerce and Logistics Technologies. These example application task domains include, but not limited to, E-Commerce data mining, recommendation systems, supply chain management, production planning, optimal control, geotechnical engineering, medical and healthcare, audio, speech and natural language processing.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MSc Systems Engineering and Engineering Management
courses.academic_group: Div of System Erg & Erg Mgt
courses.academic_org: Div of System Erg & Erg Mgt
courses.learning_outcomes: After completing this course, students will be able to: 1) understand basic concepts and principles of pattern processing and machine learning; and 2) acquire to knowledge apply statistical pattern processing and machine learning techniques in practical applications in two broad areas: Systems Engineering and Engineering Management; and E-Commerce and Logistics Technologies.
courses.course_syllabus: This graduate level course introduces the basic concepts of machine intelligence and statistical pattern processing algorithms. A particular focus is given to state-of-the-art machine learning techniques that are used in a wide range of example application systems. These techniques include, but not limited to, fundamentals of Bayes decision theory; role of data and models; classification, clustering, regression, and dimensionality reduction tasks; latent variable models; mixture of experts; expectation maximization; Markov chain and sequence modelling; variational inference; product of experts; energy based models; non-parametric modelling; decision trees; kernel methods; support vector machines; Bayesian regression and classification; and Gaussian Process. The example application systems are tailored for SEEM taught postgraduate students who specialize in one of two broad areas: Systems Engineering and Engineering Management; and E-Commerce and Logistics Technologies. These example application task domains include, but not limited to, E-Commerce data mining, recommendation systems, supply chain management, production planning, optimal control, geotechnical engineering, medical and healthcare, audio, speech and natural language processing.
courses.feedback_evaluation:
Feedback from students: CTE

Students' performance in their homework and project presentation

2. Richard O. Duda, Peter E. Hart and David G. Stork, Pattern Classification, Wiley, 2001.

3. David J. C. Mackay, Information Theory, Inference and Learning Algorithms, Cambridge University Press, 2004.
4. Kevin P. Murphy, Machine Learning: a Probabilistic Perspective, the MIT Press, 2012.
5. David Barber, Bayesian Reasoning and Machine Learning, Cambridge University Press, 2012.
6. Ian Goodfellow, Yoshua Bengio and Aaron Courville, Deep Learning, the MIT Press, 2016.

courses.description: This course is designed to put theory into practice on issues from various fields including Logistics Management, Information Technology , and Financial Engineering and so on. With company visits and field studies being required, stuents are encouraged to go out of the textbooks, and go out of the campus on the same time. Accordingly, the course includes two parts: company visits and project work with optional topics. For the company visit activities, students are organized to visit different kinds of companies and enterprises, such as bank, logistics company, insurance carrier, airline, retailer company, and government organizations, to help them understand different business operating environments and their approaches for solution development. For the project work part, under the supervision of the course instructor, students in groups will carry out a practical project in Systems Engineering & Engineering Management, by building models on practical problems, performing quantitative analysis and making decisions.

courses.subject: SEEM
courses.title: Systems Engineering and Engineering Management Internship

meetings.instructor:
Dr. PAN Hong, 
Professor HE Xuedong

courses.description:
The objective of the course is to allow students to acquire a basic understanding and the skills of the practical aspects of the Systems Engineering and Engineering Management. To qualify for the award of the subject credits, the student must attach to a company in Systems Engineering and Engineering Management related post as approved by the Professor-in-Charge for no less than 12 weeks. The student will have an academic supervisor as assigned by the Professor-in-Charge and an industry supervisor from the company. There will be a mid-term company visit by the academic supervisor. At the end of the internship, the student must give a presentation to the academic and industry supervisors, and submit a report summarizing what the student has done and learnt from the internship. The student's grade will be determined by (1) the presentation, (2) the student report and (3) a testimonial from the industry supervisor.

The internship should normally take place in the summer term after a student has finished the first two semesters of studies. Part-time students can decide to undertake the internship in the summer term of either the first or second year of studies.

Students are recommended to seek the Professor-in-Charge's comment on potential internship opportunities before enrolling in the course.
